
## 4.3 Block Diagram: Joint Variable Flow in D-H Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Joint Variable q1"] --> B["Compute T01 D-H Matrix"]
    B --> C["Joint Variable q2"]
    C --> D["Compute T12 D-H Matrix"]
    D --> E["Joint Variable qn"]
    E --> F["Compute T n-1 n"]
    F --> G["Multiply All T Matrices"]
    G --> H["Final End Effector Pose T06"]
    H --> I["Position x y z"]
    H --> J["Orientation roll pitch yaw"]

    classDef var fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    class A,C,E var
    class B,D,F,G op
    class H,I,J out
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Robot Anatomical Breakdown

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Robot["6-DOF Industrial Robot"] --> Arm["Arm Section 3 DOF"]
    Robot --> Wrist["Wrist Section 3 DOF"]
    Robot --> EE["End Effector"]

    Arm --> Sh["Shoulder Joint R"]
    Arm --> El["Elbow Joint R"]
    Arm --> UA["Upper Arm Link"]
    Arm --> LA["Lower Arm Link"]

    Wrist --> R1["Roll Joint R4"]
    Wrist --> R2["Pitch Joint R5"]
    Wrist --> R3["Yaw Joint R6"]

    EE --> Gr["Gripper Mechanism"]
    EE --> Sn["Sensors"]
    EE --> Tool["Tool Mount"]

    classDef section fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFF
    classDef part fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000

    class Robot,Arm,Wrist,EE section
    class Sh,El,UA,LA,R1,R2,R3 part
    class Gr,Sn,Tool sub
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Revolute Joint. Give one example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Revolute Joint (R)** is a lower-pair kinematic joint that permits **relative rotation** between two links about a common axis, providing **1 Degree of Freedom (DOF)**. The joint variable is the angle of rotation $\theta$ measured about the fixed joint axis.
- **Mathematical representation:** The transformation matrix contains $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ terms, with $\theta$ as the only time-varying parameter.
- **Example:** The **elbow joint of a human arm** or the **hinge of a door**. In industrial robots, revolute joints are used in the shoulder, elbow, and wrist of PUMA-type articulated robots.
- **Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + DOF (1) + Example (1).

---

### Question 2: What is an End Effector? Name its two main categories. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **end effector** is the device mounted on the **wrist flange** (terminal link) of a robotic manipulator that performs the actual **physical interaction** with the workpiece or environment.
- **Two main categories:**
  1. **Grippers / Mechanical End Effectors** — grasp and manipulate objects (e.g., 2-finger gripper, vacuum suction cup, magnetic gripper).
  2. **Process End Effectors / Tools** — perform a manufacturing operation (e.g., welding torch, paint spray nozzle, drilling spindle).
- **Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Category 1 (1) + Category 2 (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of joints used in robots with neat sketches. State the DOF and joint variable for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The following are the major types of joints used in robotic manipulators:

**1. Revolute Joint (R):**
- **Description:** Allows rotation about a fixed axis.
- **DOF:** 1
- **Joint variable:** Angle $\theta$ (in radians).
- **Sketch representation:** A cylinder with a curved arrow indicating rotational direction.
- **Example:** Robot shoulder joint, door hinge.

**2. Prismatic Joint (P):**
- **Description:** Allows linear sliding/translation along a fixed axis.
- **DOF:** 1
- **Joint variable:** Linear displacement $d$ (in meters).
- **Sketch:** A rectangular block with a straight arrow.
- **Example:** Hydraulic cylinder piston, telescopic arm.

**3. Helical / Screw Joint (H):**
- **Description:** Combined rotation and translation along the same axis (like a screw).
- **DOF:** 1 (rotation and translation are coupled)
- **Joint variable:** Angular displacement $\theta$ (translation = $L\theta / 2\pi$).
- **Example:** Lead screw in CNC machines.

**4. Cylindrical Joint (C):**
- **Description:** Revolute + Prismatic, both sharing the same axis.
- **DOF:** 2
- **Joint variables:** $\theta$ and $d$.
- **Example:** Old-style drafting machine.

**5. Spherical / Ball-and-Socket Joint (S):**
- **Description:** Permits rotation about any of three concurrent axes (like a ball joint).
- **DOF:** 3
- **Joint variables:** Three independent rotation angles (e.g., roll, pitch, yaw).
- **Example:** Human shoulder, hip joint.

**6. Planar Joint (E):**
- **Description:** Translation in 2 directions + rotation perpendicular to the plane.
- **DOF:** 3
- **Joint variables:** Two translations + one rotation.
- **Example:** Planar XY table with a turntable.

> **Mark distribution:** Each joint type (1 mark × 6 = 6) + Sketches referenced (1 mark).

---

**(b)** A 6-revolute-joint industrial robot has 7 links (including the base). Using the Kutzbach criterion, verify that the robot has 6 degrees of freedom. If the robot is to lift a payload of **W = 50 N** using a 2-finger gripper with a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.4$, calculate the minimum gripping force required per finger. Use a safety factor of **SF = 2**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — DOF Verification (3 marks):**

Given: $n = 7$ links, $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints (each providing 1 DOF), $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

Using the Kutzbach spatial criterion:

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 6 \times 6 - 30 = 36 - 30 = 6
$$

**Result:** The robot has exactly **6 DOF**, confirming it can arbitrarily position and orient its end effector in 3D space.

---

**Part (ii) — Gripper Force Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $W = 50$ N, $\mu = 0.4$, $\text{SF} = 2$.

The theoretical minimum gripping force per finger:

$$
F_g = \frac{W}{2\mu} = \frac{50}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{50}{0.8} = 62.5 \text{ N}
$$

Applying the safety factor:

$$
F_{g,\text{design}} = \text{SF} \times F_g = 2 \times 62.5 = 125 \text{ N}
$$

**Result:** Each finger must apply a minimum gripping force of **125 N** to safely lift the 50 N payload with a 2× safety margin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, classify the various end effectors used in industrial robots. Compare mechanical grippers with vacuum grippers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of End Effectors:**

```
END EFFECTORS
│
├── MECHANICAL GRIPPERS
│   ├── Two-finger parallel jaw gripper
│   ├── Three-finger angular gripper
│   ├── Four-finger enveloping gripper
│   └── Compliant / Servo-grippers
│
├── VACUUM / SUCTION GRIPPERS
│   ├── Single-cup
│   ├── Multi-cup (gantry)
│   └── Bernoulli (non-contact)
│
├── MAGNETIC GRIPPERS
│   ├── Electromagnetic
│   └── Permanent magnet
│
├── PROCESS TOOLS
│   ├── Welding torch (Spot, MIG, TIG)
│   ├── Paint spray nozzle
│   ├── Drill / deburring spindle
│   └── Adhesive dispenser
│
└── SENSORY END EFFECTORS
    ├── Force-torque sensor
    ├── Vision camera
    └── Tactile sensor
```

**Comparison: Mechanical vs Vacuum Grippers**

| Feature | Mechanical Gripper | Vacuum Gripper |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Gripping mechanism** | Physical contact + friction | Suction (negative pressure) |
| **Surface requirement** | Any rigid surface | Smooth, non-porous, flat surface |
| **Force control** | Precise via servo control | Limited; depends on vacuum level |
| **Cycle time** | 0.5–2 s (fingers open/close) | 0.1–0.5 s (faster) |
| **Payload range** | 0.1 kg – 1500 kg | 0.5 kg – 250 kg (per cup) |
| **Object damage risk** | Moderate (contact stress) | Low (distributed suction) |
| **Power consumption** | Moderate (actuators) | High (vacuum pump) |
| **Typical use** | Heavy parts, varied geometry | Sheet metal, glass, PCB panels |

> **Mark distribution:** Block diagram (3) + Comparison table (4).

---

**(b)** A cylindrical robot is configured as RPP (1 revolute + 2 prismatic). It has 4 links including the base. Compute its DOF using the Kutzbach criterion. If a force-torque sensor mounted at the wrist reads **F = 20 N** along the z-axis and a moment **M = 5 Nm** about the x-axis, determine the magnitudes of the resultant force and the resultant moment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — DOF Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $n = 4$ links, $j_1 = 3$ joints (1 revolute + 2 prismatic, each 1 DOF).

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(4 - 1) - 5(3) = 18 - 15 = 3
$$

**Result:** The RPP robot has **3 DOF** (1 rotation + 2 translations). A 3-DOF wrist would be required separately for full 6-DOF operation.

---

**Part (ii) — Resultant Force and Moment (4 marks):**

Since the sensor reads only a single component each, the magnitudes are:

**Resultant Force:**

$$
F_R = \sqrt{F_z^2} = F_z = 20 \text{ N}
$$

(If multiple components existed: $F_R = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$.)

**Resultant Moment:**

$$
M_R = \sqrt{M_x^2} = M_x = 5 \text{ Nm}
$$

(If multiple components existed: $M_R = \sqrt{M_x^2 + M_y^2 + M_z^2}$.)

**Result:** $F_R = \mathbf{20\ N}$, $M_R = \mathbf{5\ Nm}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always append **N** to forces and **Nm** to moments. A unit-less final answer loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong formula for DOF:** Students often confuse the planar **Gruebler** (coefficient 3) with the spatial **Kutzbach** (coefficient 6). Read the question carefully — 3D robots always use **Kutzbach**.
> 3. **No safety factor in gripper design:** Theoretical force alone is unacceptable in industrial design. Always multiply by **SF = 2 to 3** to score full marks.
> 4. **Confusing "joint variable" with "joint parameter":** $\theta$ and $d$ are **variables** (time-varying), while $a$, $\alpha$, and link offsets are **constant geometric parameters** (set by mechanical design).
> 5. **Skipping sketches in joint type questions:** Even a rough pencil sketch of a cylinder + arrow for a revolute joint earns the **drawing mark** (typically 1 mark per sketch in a 7-mark question).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Joints & End Effectors**

- ✅ **Revolute (R):** Rotation, 1 DOF, variable = $\theta$. Used in articulated arms.
- ✅ **Prismatic (P):** Linear slide, 1 DOF, variable = $d$. Used in Cartesian/gantry robots.
- ✅ **Spherical (S):** 3 concurrent rotations, 3 DOF. Used in wrists and human joints.
- ✅ **Cylindrical (C):** R + P coaxial, 2 DOF.
- ✅ **Planar (E):** 2 translations + 1 rotation, 3 DOF.
- ✅ **Helical (H):** Coupled rotation + translation, 1 DOF.
- ✅ **6-DOF rule:** A robot needs **≥ 6 DOF** for arbitrary position + orientation in 3D.
- ✅ **Kutzbach formula (spatial):** $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - \ldots$
- ✅ **Gruebler formula (planar):** $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- ✅ **Common robot configs:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA (RRP + 1).
- ✅ **End effectors** = **Grippers** (mechanical, vacuum, magnetic) + **Tools** (welding, spraying, drilling) + **Sensors** (force, vision, tactile).
- ✅ **Gripper force formula:** $F_g = \dfrac{W}{2\mu}$, then **multiply by safety factor (2–3)** for design.
- ✅ **Spherical wrist:** All 3 axes intersect → enables **kinematic decoupling** of position and orientation problems.
- ✅ **Redundant robot:** DOF > 6 → used for obstacle avoidance and singularity escape (e.g., 7-DOF KUKA LWR).
- ✅ **KTU-style answer format:** Always write **Definition → DOF → Joint variable → Example → Sketch** for full marks on joint-type questions.
- ✅ **Industrial standard gripping force range:** **10 N – 1000 N**; payload **0.5 kg – 1500 kg**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanisms and Manipulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT743 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanisms and Manipulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Mechanism?

A **mechanism** is an assembly of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints, designed to transmit motion and/or force in a predetermined manner. In robotics, a mechanism forms the mechanical skeleton of the robot that enables controlled relative motion between its constituent parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *mechanism* is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected by kinematic pairs (joints) whose purpose is to convert, transmit, or modify motion. In robotics, mechanisms are the foundational building blocks that define the robot's kinematic and dynamic capabilities.

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent parameters that define the configuration (position and orientation) of a mechanism in space is known as its **degrees of freedom**. A rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 DOF** — three translational and three rotational.

## 1.2 What is a Manipulator?

A **robotic manipulator** is a programmable, multifunctional mechanism designed to grasp, move, manipulate materials, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *manipulator* in robotics refers specifically to the **arm-and-wrist assembly** (excluding the end-effector) that performs the reaching, positioning, and orienting functions. It is essentially a chain of rigid links interconnected by joints forming an open or closed kinematic chain.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine your own human arm as a perfect biological manipulator:

- **Shoulder joint** → like a **revolute joint** (rotates like a door hinge in multiple planes).
- **Elbow joint** → another **revolute joint** (allows bending).
- **Wrist** → a **spherical joint** (roll, pitch, yaw).
- **Upper arm, forearm, palm** → the rigid **links** of the manipulator.
- **Fingers holding a pen** → the **end-effector** at the distal end of the chain.

In robotics, we mathematically mimic this biological marvel by chaining rigid links with joints — a manipulator is, quite literally, a "mechanical arm."

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition | KTU Board Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Link** | A rigid body component of the mechanism | Rigid member |
| **Joint / Kinematic Pair** | Connection between two links allowing relative motion | Joint |
| **Base** | The fixed link of the manipulator | Frame 0 |
| **End-Effector** | Device at the tip of the manipulator (gripper, tool) | Tool |
| **Workspace** | Set of all points reachable by the end-effector | Reach envelope |
| **Kinematic Chain** | A series of links connected by joints | Open/closed chain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **standard** for measuring robot reachability is the **workspace volume** in cubic meters, typically expressed in **m³** or **mm³** for precision assemblies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Joints (Kinematic Pairs)

A **kinematic pair** is a joint between two links that constrains their relative motion. The number of DOF a joint allows is critical for robot design.

### Lower Pairs (Surface Contact — Most Common in Robotics)

- **Revolute (R) Joint** — Rotation about a single axis. **DOF = 1**. *Example:* Hinge, robot shoulder.
- **Prismatic (P) Joint** — Translation along a single axis. **DOF = 1**. *Example:* Slider, hydraulic piston.
- **Cylindrical Joint** — Combined rotation and translation along the same axis. **DOF = 2**.
- **Spherical (Ball-and-Socket) Joint** — Rotation about three intersecting axes. **DOF = 3**.
- **Planar Joint** — Translation in two axes and rotation about the third. **DOF = 3**.
- **Screw (Helical) Joint** — Rotation and translation coupled along same axis. **DOF = 1**.

### Higher Pairs (Point/Line Contact)

- Cam-follower, gear pair, ball bearing — rarely used as primary robot joints.

## 2.2 Open vs. Closed Kinematic Chains

| Property | Open Chain | Closed Chain |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Links connected in series (one end free) | Loops present; both ends fixed |
| **Workspace** | Large, dexterous | Smaller, stiffer |
| **Examples** | Articulated arm, SCARA | Parallel manipulator (Delta, Stewart platform) |
| **Error accumulation** | High (errors add up at each joint) | Low (errors distributed) |
| **Stiffness** | Lower | Higher |

## 2.3 Gruebler's Criterion for Planar Mechanisms

For a **planar mechanism**, the mobility (M) is given by:

$$
M \;=\; 3(n-1) \;-\; 2j_1 \;-\; j_2
$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints)

## 2.4 Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion for Spatial Mechanisms

For a **spatial (3D) mechanism**, the DOF is given by:

$$
M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; 5j_1 \;-\; 4j_2 \;-\; 3j_3 \;-\; 2j_4 \;-\; j_5
$$

where:
- $n$ = number of links (including ground)
- $j_i$ = number of joints with $i$ degrees of freedom

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Special Note:** For most industrial robots, all joints are either revolute (R) or prismatic (P), both of which have **1 DOF**. So the simplified form $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1$ is the workhorse equation for manipulator analysis.

## 2.5 Classification of Robot Configurations

| Configuration | Joint Notation | DOF | DOF Justification | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian (PPP)** | 3 prismatic | 3 | Pure translation, no rotation | Pick-and-place, CNC loading |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | 1R + 2P | 3 | Rotation + radial lift + vertical lift | Assembly, machine tending |
| **Spherical / Polar (RRP)** | 2R + 1P | 3 | Two rotations + one translation | Welding, painting |
| **Articulated (RRR)** | 3 revolute | 3 | Three rotations, like human arm | Welding, complex assembly |
| **SCARA (RRP)** | 2R + 1P (selective compliance) | 3 (or 4) | High stiffness vertically, compliant horizontally | PCB assembly, packaging |
| **Parallel (e.g., Delta)** | Multiple closed chains | 3 or 6 | Parallel actuators | High-speed pick-and-place |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — it appears in nearly every KTU university exam question on this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Kutzbach–Grübler (spatial) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Grübler (planar) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{DOF}_{\text{rigid body, 3D}} = 6$ | Max independent motions | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{DOF}_{\text{rigid body, 2D}} = 3$ | Planar max | dimensionless |
| 5 | $W = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i^{\max} - q_i^{\min}$ | Joint-space workspace | $(\text{rad or m})^n$ |
| 6 | $V_{\text{reachable}} = f(\text{link lengths, joint limits})$ | Cartesian workspace volume | $\text{m}^3$ |

> Real-world utility: These formulas drive the design of surgical robots (high precision, multiple DOF), industrial assembly arms (3–6 DOF), and parallel manipulators used in flight simulators (6-DOF motion platforms).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a Rigid Body in 3D has 6 DOF

We start from the most basic premise: a rigid body cannot deform, but it can move in space.

**Step 1 — Position description.**
A point in 3D space requires 3 coordinates: $(x, y, z)$. So the position of any reference point on the body needs **3 parameters**.

**Step 2 — Orientation description.**
The orientation of the body is described by a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix $R$. While $R$ has 9 entries, the orthonormality constraints $R^T R = I$ give 6 constraints, leaving only **3 independent parameters** (e.g., roll, pitch, yaw — or Euler angles).

**Step 3 — Total DOF.**
The total configuration of a free rigid body is therefore:

$$
\text{DOF} \;=\; (\text{positional parameters}) + (\text{orientational parameters}) \;=\; 3 + 3 \;=\; 6
$$

**Conclusion:** A free rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 independent degrees of freedom**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Kutzbach–Grübler Equation for Spatial Mechanisms

We aim to count the mobility $M$ of a spatial mechanism in terms of links and joints.

**Step 1 — Free mobility baseline.**
If we have $n$ links, all unconnected, in 3D space, the total free mobility is:

$$
M_{\text{free}} \;=\; 6n
$$

**Step 2 — Constraint imposed by the ground link.**
One link (the ground) is fixed. The constraint it imposes is exactly the DOF of a rigid body, i.e., **6 constraints**:

$$
M_{\text{after fixing ground}} \;=\; 6n - 6 \;=\; 6(n-1)
$$

**Step 3 — Effect of joints.**
A joint between two links removes DOF. A joint with $i$ DOF removes $(6 - i)$ relative motions. For instance:
- A revolute joint ($i = 1$ DOF) imposes $6 - 1 = 5$ constraints.
- A spherical joint ($i = 3$ DOF) imposes $6 - 3 = 3$ constraints.

**Step 4 — Aggregate constraints.**
Summing constraints from all $j_i$ joints:

$$
M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6-i)\, j_i
$$

**Step 5 — Simplification.**

$$
M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; 5j_1 \;-\; 4j_2 \;-\; 3j_3 \;-\; 2j_4 \;-\; j_5
$$

This is the **Kutzbach–Grübler equation** for spatial mechanisms.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: DOF of a 3R Articulated Robot Arm

**Given:** A manipulator with $n = 4$ links (base + 3 arms) connected by 3 revolute joints.

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

$$
n = 4, \quad j_1 = 3 \ (\text{revolute joints}), \quad j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the spatial Grübler equation.**

$$
M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; 5j_1
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
M \;=\; 6(4-1) \;-\; 5(3) \;=\; 18 - 15 \;=\; 3
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**
The manipulator has **3 DOF**, meaning the end-effector can reach any point in a 3D volume (no orientation control). To get 6 DOF (full position + orientation), one typically adds a **3-DOF spherical wrist**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: DOF of a 4-DOF SCARA Robot

**Given:** SCARA robot with $n = 5$ links (base + 2 arms + wrist housing + end-effector) and joints: 2R + 1P + 1R (the final R is the end-effector roll).

**Step 1 — Count links and joints.**

$$
n = 5, \quad j_1 = 4 \ (\text{three R + one P})
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Grübler.**

$$
M \;=\; 6(5-1) \;-\; 5(4) \;=\; 24 - 20 \;=\; 4
$$

**Step 3 — Verification.**
SCARA robots are typically quoted as **4 DOF**: shoulder rotation, elbow rotation, vertical prismatic (Z-axis), and end-effector rotation (theta about Z). ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Manipulator Comparison

| Configuration | DOF | Joints | Workspace Shape | Stiffness | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | 3 | PPP | Rectangular box | High | Pick-and-place, CNC |
| Cylindrical | 3 | RPP | Hollow cylinder | Medium | Machine tending |
| Spherical/Polar | 3 | RRP | Spherical shell | Medium-low | Welding |
| Articulated | 3+3 (arm + wrist) | RRR + RRR | Sphere minus pole | Low | Complex assembly, painting |
| SCARA | 4 | RRP + R | Flat disc + thin cylinder | High in Z | PCB assembly |
| Parallel (Delta) | 3 or 6 | Multiple | Dome or platform | Very high | High-speed sorting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Kinematic Chain Structure of a 6-DOF Articulated Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Base["BASE LINK Frame 0"] -->|Joint 1 Revolute Z axis| S1["SHOULDER Link 1"]
    S1 -->|Joint 2 Revolute| UArm["UPPER ARM Link 2"]
    UArm -->|Joint 3 Revolute| Elbow["ELBOW Link 3"]
    Elbow -->|Joint 4 Revolute| FArm["FOREARM Link 4"]
    FArm -->|Joint 5 Revolute| Wrist1["WRIST 1 Link 5"]
    Wrist1 -->|Joint 6 Revolute| Wrist2["WRIST 2 Link 6"]
    Wrist2 --> EndEff["END EFFECTOR Gripper Tool"]
    Base -.-> Grounded["Ground Fixed Reference"]
    EndEff --> Workspace["Cartesian Workspace XYZ Volume"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Joint Type Classification (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Joints["KINEMATIC JOINTS"] --> Lower["Lower Pairs Surface Contact"]
    Joints --> Higher["Higher Pairs Point or Line Contact"]
    Lower --> Rev["Revolute 1 DOF Rotation"]
    Lower --> Pris["Prismatic 1 DOF Translation"]
    Lower --> Cyl["Cylindrical 2 DOF R plus T"]
    Lower --> Sph["Spherical 3 DOF Three Rotations"]
    Lower --> Planar["Planar 3 DOF 2T plus 1R"]
    Lower --> Screw["Screw Helical 1 DOF Coupled"]
    Higher --> CamF["Cam Follower Pair"]
    Higher --> GearP["Gear Pair"]
    Higher --> BallB["Ball Bearing Race"]
    Rev --> R1["R notation R axis"]
    Pris --> P1["P notation Linear axis"]
    Screw --> H1["H notation Helical axis"]
    Cyl --> C1["C notation Combined"]
    Sph --> S1["S notation Three rotational axes"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — DOF Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Identify Mechanism Type"] --> Q1{"Planar or Spatial"}
    Q1 -->|Planar| PlanEq["Use M equals 3n minus 1 minus 2j1 minus j2"]
    Q1 -->|Spatial| SpatEq["Use M equals 6n minus 1 minus 5j1 minus 4j2 minus 3j3 minus 2j4 minus j5"]
    PlanEq --> Count1["Count n total links"]
    SpatEq --> Count2["Count n total links including ground"]
    Count1 --> Class1["Classify each joint by its DOF value"]
    Count2 --> Class2["Classify each joint by its DOF value"]
    Class1 --> Sub1["Substitute into planar equation"]
    Class2 --> Sub2["Substitute into spatial equation"]
    Sub1 --> Result1["Result M DOF of mechanism"]
    Sub2 --> Result2["Result M DOF of mechanism"]
    Result1 --> Check1["M greater than 0 implies mobility is positive"]
    Result2 --> Check2["M equals 0 implies structure is a mechanism"]
    Check1 --> Ind{"M value"}
    Check2 --> Ind
    Ind -->|M equals 0| Mech["Mechanism proper motion constrained"]
    Ind -->|M greater than 0| Mob["Mobile manipulator with controlled DOF"]
    Ind -->|M less than 0| Str["Structure statically indeterminate"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Robot Configuration Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Config["ROBOT CONFIGURATIONS"] --> Cart["Cartesian PPP XYZ"]
    Config --> Cyl["Cylindrical RPP R plus Z plus Theta"]
    Config --> Sph["Spherical Polar RRP R R plus Z"]
    Config --> Art["Articulated RRR R plus R plus R"]
    Config --> SCARA["SCARA RRP R Selective Compliance"]
    Config --> Para["Parallel Delta Stewart Multiple Chains"]
    Cart --> WS1["Workspace Rectangular Box"]
    Cyl --> WS2["Workspace Hollow Cylinder"]
    Sph --> WS3["Workspace Spherical Shell"]
    Art --> WS4["Workspace Sphere Minus Singularity"]
    SCARA --> WS5["Workspace Flat Disc Plus Vertical Lift"]
    Para --> WS6["Workspace Dome or Hexapod Platform"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "degree of freedom" of a robot manipulator. State the number of degrees of freedom of a rigid body in 3D space.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **degree of freedom (DOF)** of a robot manipulator is the number of independent parameters (joint variables) that must be specified to completely describe the configuration of the manipulator in space.

A rigid body in 3D space has **6 degrees of freedom**: 3 translational ($x, y, z$) and 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw).

To position and orient a rigid body in 3D space completely, all 6 DOF must be specified. Hence, a general-purpose industrial robot arm requires a minimum of 6 joints to achieve full position and orientation control of its end-effector.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — 3D rigid body DOF = 6 with explanation of 3+3 split, 1 mark — practical implication for robot design.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between open kinematic chain and closed kinematic chain manipulators with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Open Kinematic Chain | Closed Kinematic Chain |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Links connected in series; one end fixed (base), other end free (end-effector) | Forms one or more closed loops; multiple links connect back to base |
| **Stiffness** | Lower stiffness; longer chains flex | Higher stiffness; load distributed |
| **Workspace** | Larger, more dexterous | Smaller, more constrained |
| **Error accumulation** | Errors accumulate along the chain | Errors are averaged/cancelled in loops |
| **Example** | Articulated arm (e.g., ABB IRB, KUKA KR series) | Delta robot, Stewart platform |

*(1 mark — definition of each, 1 mark — comparison points, 1 mark — examples.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]**

**(a)** State the Kutzbach–Grübler criterion for determining the degrees of freedom of a spatial mechanism. Explain each term with a neat sketch showing the joint types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF articulated robot manipulator has the following joint sequence: **R-R-R-R-R-R** (all revolute). The link lengths are $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m, and $L_3 = 0.3$ m. Compute the total reach (maximum and minimum distance) of the end-effector from the base, ignoring joint limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion:**

$$
M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; 5j_1 \;-\; 4j_2 \;-\; 3j_3 \;-\; 2j_4 \;-\; j_5
$$

**Term explanations:**

- $M$ = Mobility (DOF) of the mechanism.
- $n$ = Total number of links, including the fixed ground link.
- $j_1$ = Number of 1-DOF joints (revolute, prismatic, screw).
- $j_2$ = Number of 2-DOF joints (e.g., cylindrical joint, when not in series).
- $j_3$ = Number of 3-DOF joints (e.g., spherical joint).
- $j_4$ = Number of 4-DOF joints (e.g., planar joint, when isolated).
- $j_5$ = Number of 5-DOF joints.

**Sketch of joint types (textual representation):**

```
Revolute (R):     |  o  |  link rotates about z
Prismatic (P):   |--->  |  link slides along x
Cylindrical:     |  ⟲--> |  rotation + translation along same axis
Spherical:        \  o  /  three rotations
```

**Why it works:** A free link in 3D has 6 DOF. With $n$ links we have $6n$ free DOF. The ground fixes 6 DOF, and each joint with $i$ DOF imposes $(6-i)$ constraints. Summing gives the equation.

*(3 marks — equation, 2 marks — term explanations, 2 marks — sketch/justification.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** 3-link planar arm (ignoring wrist joints for reach calculation).
- $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m, $L_3 = 0.3$ m.
- Assume all joints can rotate through $360°$ (no joint limits).

**Step 1 — Maximum reach.**
Maximum reach occurs when all links are aligned in a straight line:

$$
R_{\max} \;=\; L_1 + L_2 + L_3
$$

$$
R_{\max} \;=\; 0.4 + 0.5 + 0.3 \;=\; 1.2 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum reach.**
Minimum reach (excluding the base) occurs when the arm folds back on itself. The minimum is:

$$
R_{\min} \;=\; \vert L_1 - (L_2 + L_3) \vert \quad \text{or} \quad \vert L_1 + L_2 - L_3 \vert \quad \text{whichever is smaller}
$$

Computing both:

$$
\vert L_1 - (L_2 + L_3) \vert \;=\; \vert 0.4 - 0.8 \vert \;=\; 0.4 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\vert (L_1 + L_2) - L_3 \vert \;=\; \vert 0.9 - 0.3 \vert \;=\; 0.6 \ \text{m}
$$

The smaller value is **0.4 m**.

**Step 3 — Result.**

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} = 1.2 \ \text{m}, \quad R_{\min} = 0.4 \ \text{m}}
$$

The end-effector can reach any distance $r$ in the range $0.4 \leq r \leq 1.2$ m from the base.

*(2 marks — setup, 2 marks — $R_{\max}$ calculation, 2 marks — $R_{\min}$ calculation, 1 mark — final answer and interpretation.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic structure of a robotic manipulator. Identify and label the major sub-systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical coordinate robot has a horizontal reach of 1.5 m and a vertical stroke of 0.8 m. The base rotation is unrestricted. Calculate: (i) the maximum vertical reach above the base, (ii) the maximum horizontal reach, and (iii) the volume of the cylindrical workspace. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A robotic manipulator consists of the following major sub-systems:

```
+----------------------------------------------------+
|                ROBOT MANIPULATOR                   |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
|  [1] BASE / PEDESTAL — Houses joint 1, mounting.   |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [2] SHOULDER & ARM LINKS — Provides reach.        |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [3] WRIST ASSEMBLY — Provides orientation.        |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [4] END-EFFECTOR / GRIPPER — Interacts with work. |
|         |                                          |
|         v                                          |
|  [5] ACTUATORS — Servo motors / hydraulic pistons. |
|  [6] SENSORS — Encoders, force/torque sensors.     |
|  [7] CONTROLLER — Computes joint trajectories.     |
+----------------------------------------------------+
```

**Key sub-systems explained:**

1. **Mechanical structure (links + joints)** — the physical arm.
2. **Actuators** — provide the driving force (electric, hydraulic, pneumatic).
3. **Sensors** — provide feedback on position, velocity, force.
4. **Controller** — generates motion commands.
5. **End-effector** — the tool/gripper that performs the actual task.

*(2 marks — block diagram, 2 marks — labels, 3 marks — sub-system explanation.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** Cylindrical robot.
- Horizontal reach: $r = 1.5$ m.
- Vertical stroke: $h = 0.8$ m.
- Base rotation: $0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi$ (unrestricted).

**Step 1 — Maximum vertical reach.**
For a cylindrical robot, the vertical axis $Z$ is independent of the radial reach. So:

$$
Z_{\max} = h = 0.8 \ \text{m above the base}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum horizontal reach.**

$$
r_{\max} = 1.5 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Workspace volume.**
A cylindrical coordinate robot sweeps a **hollow cylinder** (since $r$ usually has a minimum too, often $r_{\min} = 0$ for simplicity). With unrestricted rotation, the swept volume is:

$$
V \;=\; \pi \cdot r_{\max}^2 \cdot h
$$

$$
V \;=\; \pi \cdot (1.5)^2 \cdot (0.8) \;=\; \pi \cdot 2.25 \cdot 0.8 \;=\; 1.8\pi
$$

$$
\boxed{V \;\approx\; 5.6549 \ \text{m}^3}
$$

*(2 marks — vertical reach, 2 marks — horizontal reach, 2 marks — volume formula, 1 mark — final numeric answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to count the ground link** in $n$ — this is the #1 reason students lose marks on Grübler's equation.
> 2. **Using the wrong equation** (planar vs spatial) — for 3D robots, always use the **6(n−1) − 5j₁ − 4j₂ − ...** form.
> 3. **Confusing $R_{\min}$ and $R_{\max}$** in reach problems — minimum is the folded-back configuration, not zero (unless the arm can reach the base).
> 4. **Workspace volume units** — always express in $\text{m}^3$, not $\text{m}^2$.
> 5. **Joint notation** — examiners are strict: R, P, C, S, H must be written in the correct case and order.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **mechanism** = links + joints converting motion. A **manipulator** = mechanism used to grasp/move objects.
- A rigid body in 3D has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Kutzbach–Grübler (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- **Grübler (planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- Common robot configurations: **Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA (RRP+R), Parallel (Delta/Stewart)**.
- A 3-DOF arm gives **position only**; adding a 3-DOF spherical wrist gives **full 6-DOF position + orientation**.
- **Workspace** for cylindrical robot: $V = \pi r_{\max}^2 h$.
- **Maximum reach** = sum of link lengths; **Minimum reach** = smallest folded-back distance.
- **Open chain** = serial links, larger workspace, less stiff.
- **Closed chain** = parallel/looped links, smaller workspace, stiffer, lower error.
- Lower pairs (R, P) dominate robot design because they are **self-locking, low-wear, and high-load**.
- Always **include the ground link** when counting $n$ in Grübler's formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT743 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degree of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of robotics and automation, the **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a mechanical system is defined as the **number of independent parameters (coordinates)** required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of the system in space, or equivalently, the number of independent relative motions that the links of a mechanism can execute without violating any constraints imposed by the joints.

For a rigid body in three-dimensional space, the **maximum DOF is six (6)**, comprising:
- **3 translational DOF** (motion along $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes)
- **3 rotational DOF** (rotation about $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes — roll, pitch, yaw)

In a planar (two-dimensional) system, the maximum DOF reduces to **three (3)**: two translations ($x$, $y$) and one rotation ($\theta$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Degree of Freedom is the foundational concept upon which the entire kinematic analysis of manipulators is built. Every joint type contributes a specific, discrete number of DOF, and the total DOF of a robotic system is the sum of contributions from its joints, minus any redundant constraints.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your **human arm**. If you focus only on the shoulder, you can move it **forward-backward, sideways, and rotate it in a circle** — that is **3 DOF** at the shoulder joint alone. The elbow adds **1 more DOF** (flexion/extension). The wrist adds another **3 DOF** (pitch, yaw, roll). Adding the fingers and thumb pushes the total beyond 20 DOF. This is why you can reach for a coffee cup, rotate it to fit your hand, and pour it without spilling — your biological manipulator is highly dexterous because it has many degrees of freedom.

For industrial robots:
- A **SCARA robot** typically has **4 DOF** (suited for pick-and-place on a plane)
- A **6-DOF articulated arm** (like the PUMA or ABB IRB series) is the **industry standard** for tasks requiring arbitrary positioning and orientation in 3D space
- A **7-DOF redundant manipulator** adds one extra joint to allow the robot to reach a point with multiple elbow configurations (avoiding obstacles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** More DOF means greater flexibility and dexterity, but it also means more complex kinematics, higher cost, and more challenging control algorithms.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Physical Meaning |
|--------|:------:|:--------------:|------------------|
| Number of links | $n$ | Integer | Total number of rigid bodies in the mechanism |
| Lower pair joints (1 DOF) | $j_1$ | Integer | Revolute (R), Prismatic (P) |
| Higher pair joints (2 DOF) | $j_2$ | Integer | Cam-follower, gear mesh |
| Joint DOF contribution | varies | 1 to 5 | Depends on joint class |
| Mobility (Mobility of mechanism) | $M$ or $DOF$ | Real number | Net independent motions |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The Degree of Freedom calculation in any robotic manipulator or mechanical linkage relies on **topological analysis** of the kinematic chain. A kinematic chain is a sequence of rigid links connected by joints, forming a constrained system. To determine DOF, we must systematically account for:

1. **The base reference frame** — one link is grounded and treated as stationary.
2. **Joint constraints** — each joint removes certain degrees of freedom (a revolute joint removes 5 DOF in 3D space, allowing only 1 rotational motion).
3. **Rigid body assumptions** — links do not deform.

### 2.2 Gruebler's Equation (Planar Mechanisms)

For mechanisms operating in a **2D plane**, the mobility is given by:

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

where:
- $M$ = mobility (degree of freedom)
- $n$ = total number of links (including the ground link)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (joints with 1 DOF — revolute or prismatic)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (joints with 2 DOF — point/line contact like cams, gears)

### 2.3 Kutzbach Criterion (Spatial Mechanisms)

For **3D spatial mechanisms**, the mobility is given by:

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

where:
- $j_1$ = number of joints with 1 DOF (R or P)
- $j_2$ = number of joints with 2 DOF (e.g., cylindrical without rotation lock)
- $j_3$ = number of joints with 3 DOF (e.g., spherical)
- $j_4$ = number of joints with 4 DOF
- $j_5$ = number of joints with 5 DOF

### 2.4 Joint Classification Reference

| Joint Type | Symbol | DOF (Spatial) | DOF (Planar) | Allowed Motion |
|:----------:|:------:|:-------------:|:------------:|----------------|
| Revolute | R | 1 | 1 | Rotation about a single axis |
| Prismatic | P | 1 | 1 | Translation along a single axis |
| Cylindrical | C | 2 | 2 | Rotation + translation along same axis |
| Spherical / Ball-and-Socket | S | 3 | — | 3 independent rotations |
| Planar | E | 3 | 3 | 2 translations + 1 rotation in plane |
| Screw / Helical | H | 1 | — | Coupled rotation and translation |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Application | Variables | Units |
|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Planar mechanisms | $n, j_1, j_2$ | Dimensionless |
| $M = 6(n-1) - \sum k_i \cdot j_i$ | Spatial mechanisms | $n, j_i, k_i$ | Dimensionless |
| $DOF_{robot} = \sum DOF_{joints}$ | Serial manipulator | Joint DOFs | Dimensionless |
| $\lambda = 6n - \sum (6 - f_i) \cdot j_i$ | General Kutzbach (alternative form) | $n, j_i, f_i$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The DOF calculation is not academic — it is the first critical design check. If a robot arm has **zero or negative mobility**, the mechanism is a structure (cannot move) and the design is fundamentally flawed. If the DOF is **exactly 1**, it is a function generator. If DOF **exceeds the required task complexity**, it is redundant and requires advanced control.

### 2.6 Special Cases and Singularities

- **$M = 0$:** Structure — no motion possible (over-constrained design)
- **$M = 1$:** Function generator — input uniquely determines output
- **$M = 2$:** Path generator — output traces a curve
- **$M > 0$ but unexpected:** The mechanism may be under-constrained or contain a **redundant DOF**
- **Special configurations:** Even with correct global DOF, certain joint alignments can cause **local singularities** where instantaneous motion is lost (e.g., when two revolute joint axes become collinear)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Gruebler's Equation (Planar)

#### Step 1: Define the Free Bodies

Consider a planar mechanism with $n$ rigid links. If all links were **completely free** (no joints, no constraints) in the 2D plane, the total degrees of freedom would be:

$$
DO_{free} = 3n
$$

Each rigid body in 2D possesses **3 independent motions**: translation in $x$, translation in $y$, and rotation $\theta$.

#### Step 2: Subtract the Fixed Link

One link is designated as the **ground (fixed) link**. Since the ground does not move, we must subtract its 3 DOF:

$$
DO_{unconstrained} = 3n - 3 = 3(n - 1)
$$

#### Step 3: Account for Joint Constraints

Each joint between two links **removes certain independent motions**:
- A **lower pair joint** (revolute or prismatic) in 2D provides **2 constraints** (it allows only 1 DOF, so it removes 2 from the 3 possible).
- A **higher pair joint** provides **1 constraint** in 2D (it allows 2 DOF, removing only 1).

Therefore, the net DOF (mobility $M$) is:

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - 1 \cdot j_2
$$

#### Step 4: Final Form

Rearranging in standard textbook notation:

$$
\boxed{M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2}
$$

This is **Gruebler's formula** for planar mechanisms.

### 3.2 Derivation of Kutzbach Criterion (Spatial)

#### Step 1: Free Bodies in 3D

For $n$ rigid bodies in 3D space, each has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). Total unconstrained DOF:

$$
DO_{free} = 6n
$$

#### Step 2: Ground Link Removal

Removing 6 DOF for the fixed link:

$$
DO_{unconstrained} = 6(n - 1)
$$

#### Step 3: Joint Constraint Subtraction

A joint with $f$ degrees of freedom imposes $(6 - f)$ constraints. For each joint type:

| Joint | DOF $f_i$ | Constraints $(6 - f_i)$ |
|:-----:|:--------:|:----------------------:|
| 1-DOF (R or P) | 1 | 5 |
| 2-DOF | 2 | 4 |
| 3-DOF (S) | 3 | 3 |
| 4-DOF | 4 | 2 |
| 5-DOF | 5 | 1 |

#### Step 4: Aggregate Constraint Subtraction

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1 \cdot j_5
$$

#### Step 5: Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{M = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6 - i) \cdot j_i}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: 4-Bar Planar Linkage

**Given:** A planar four-bar mechanism has 4 links (including ground) connected by 4 revolute joints.

**Step 1: Identify variables**

We have $n = 4$ and $j_1 = 4$ (all revolute are lower pairs in planar case), $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 2: Apply Gruebler's equation**

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$
M = 3 \cdot 3 - 8
$$

$$
M = 9 - 8
$$

$$
\boxed{M = 1}
$$

**Interpretation:** The four-bar linkage has **1 DOF** — this matches physical intuition: driving one link (the input crank) determines the motion of all other links uniquely. This is the classic **function generator** mechanism used in countless machines.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Spatial 6R Robot Manipulator

**Given:** A PUMA-type industrial robot has 6 revolute joints and 7 links (including ground).

**Step 1: Identify variables**

$n = 7$, all joints are revolute so $j_1 = 6$, and $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

**Step 2: Apply Kutzbach criterion**

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
M = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6)
$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$
M = 6 \cdot 6 - 30
$$

$$
M = 36 - 30
$$

$$
\boxed{M = 6}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 6R manipulator has **6 DOF** — exactly the number needed to position and orient an end-effector arbitrarily in 3D space (3 translations + 3 rotations).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
