- Compare reference with measured output: $e(t) = r(t) - H(s) \cdot y(t)$.
- Compute control signal: $u(t) = C(s) \cdot e(t)$.
- Drive plant: $y(t) = G(s) \cdot u(t)$.
- Sensor measures: $y_m(t) = H(s) \cdot y(t)$.
- Loop continues until error $\to 0$ (in steady state for a properly designed system).

**Engineering Utility:**
- **High accuracy**, **disturbance rejection**, **robustness** to model uncertainty.
- Essential for **precision robotics, manufacturing automation, surgical robots, autonomous vehicles**.

---

### 2.3 Closed-Loop Transfer Function Derivation (Generic)

For a unity feedback system $H(s) = 1$:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$
$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot E(s)$$

Substituting:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot [R(s) - Y(s)]$$
$$Y(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) C(s)] = G(s) C(s) R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function** (CLTF).

For **non-unity feedback** $H(s) \neq 1$:

$$\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s) H(s)}$$

---

### 2.4 Control Laws — Detailed Mathematical Description

#### 2.4.1 P-Controller (Proportional)

**Control Law:**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$

**Laplace Form:**

$$C(s) = K_p$$

- $K_p$ = Proportional gain.
- **Effect:** Reduces rise time, increases system speed, but introduces **steady-state error** and may cause **oscillation** if $K_p$ is too high.

#### 2.4.2 PD-Controller (Proportional-Derivative)

**Control Law:**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Laplace Form:**

$$C(s) = K_p + K_d s$$

- $K_d$ = Derivative gain.
- **Effect:** Adds **damping**, reduces overshoot and oscillation, **anticipates** future error based on its rate of change. Does **not eliminate** steady-state error.
- **Caveat:** Derivative amplifies **noise** — a low-pass filter is often added.

#### 2.4.3 PID-Controller (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)

**Control Law:**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Laplace Form:**

$$C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

- $K_i$ = Integral gain.
- **Effect:** The **integral term** accumulates past error → eliminates **steady-state error**. The combination delivers fast, accurate, well-damped response.
- **Standard form (with $K_p$ factored):**

$$C(s) = K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$$

Where $T_i = K_p / K_i$ and $T_d = K_d / K_p$.

---

### 2.5 Linear vs Non-Linear Control — Formal Criteria

**Linearity Test — Superposition:**

A system is linear **iff** for all inputs $u_1$, $u_2$ and scalars $a$, $b$:

$$y_{combined}(t) = a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t)$$

**Linearity of the Three Controllers:**

| Controller | Linear? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| P | ✅ Yes | $u = K_p e$ is a linear operator on $e$. |
| PD | ✅ Yes | Sum of linear operations. |
| PID | ✅ Yes | All three terms are linear in $e$. |

**Note:** PID is **linear** as a control law, but when applied to a **non-linear plant** (e.g., a multi-link robot manipulator), the overall closed-loop system becomes **non-linear**.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop output | $y(t) = G(s) u(t)$ | $G(s)$ is plant TF |
| 2 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | — |
| 3 | Unity-feedback CLTF | $Y(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{G(s)C(s)}{1 + G(s)C(s)}$ | Standard form |
| 4 | Non-unity feedback CLTF | $Y(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$ | $H(s)$ = sensor TF |
| 5 | Error TF | $E(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)C(s)}$ | For $H(s)=1$ |
| 6 | P-controller | $C(s) = K_p$ | $K_p > 0$ |
| 7 | PD-controller | $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$ | Adds damping |
| 8 | PID-controller | $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ | Eliminates SSE |
| 9 | Steady-state error (step input) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ (P only) | — |
| 10 | Steady-state error (PID) | $e_{ss} = 0$ (for step) | Integral term kills SSE |
| 11 | Characteristic equation | $1 + G(s)C(s) = 0$ | Roots determine stability |
| 12 | DC motor TF (position) | $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K}{s(Ls + R)(Js + b) + K^2}$ | $\theta$ = angle |
| 13 | Linearity test | $G\{au_1 + bu_2\} = aG\{u_1\} + bG\{u_2\}$ | Must hold for all $a,b,u_1,u_2$ |
| 14 | Non-linearity examples | Saturation, dead-zone, Coulomb friction, $x^2$ terms | Inherently non-LTI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **unity-feedback CLTF** and the **three controller transfer functions**. They appear in almost every Module 4 question.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Concept |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Robotics** | PID joint servo loops in 6-DOF arms (KUKA, ABB, Fanuc). |
| **CNC Machining** | Closed-loop position control for sub-micron accuracy. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | PID steering + lateral position control. |
| **Drones / UAVs** | Cascade PID for roll/pitch/yaw stabilization. |
| **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)** | High-gain PD/PID for tremor-free motion. |
| **Humanoid Robots** | Non-linear control (computed-torque) for coupled dynamics. |
| **Process Control** | PID in chemical, thermal, and pressure regulation. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Unity Feedback)

**Given:** A unity negative feedback system with forward path $G(s) C(s)$ and feedback path $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 1 — Define the system equations from the block diagram:**

The error signal at the summing junction is the reference minus the measured output:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$

**Step 2 — Control law (controller output):**

The controller $C(s)$ acts on the error to produce the control input to the plant:

$$U(s) = C(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Plant response:**

The plant $G(s)$ converts the control signal into the physical output:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot U(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the error equation from Step 1:**

$$Y(s) = G(s) C(s) \cdot [R(s) - Y(s)]$$

**Step 6 — Expand and collect $Y(s)$ terms on the left side:**

$$Y(s) = G(s) C(s) R(s) - G(s) C(s) Y(s)$$
$$Y(s) + G(s) C(s) Y(s) = G(s) C(s) R(s)$$
$$Y(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) C(s)] = G(s) C(s) R(s)$$

**Step 7 — Isolate the ratio $Y(s)/R(s)$:**

$$\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}$$

> This is the **closed-loop transfer function** for a unity-feedback system. The denominator $1 + G(s)C(s)$ is called the **characteristic polynomial**, and its roots determine the system's stability.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Transfer Function of an Armature-Controlled DC Motor (Position Output)

The DC motor is the most common actuator in robotics, and deriving its transfer function is a **KTU favorite**.

**Step 1 — Electrical equation (armature circuit, KVL):**

The applied armature voltage $V_a(s)$ drives current $I_a(s)$ through resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$, and induces a back-EMF $E_b(s)$:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + E_b(s)$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF relation:**

The back-EMF is proportional to the angular velocity $\omega(s) = s \theta(s)$:

$$E_b(s) = K_b \cdot \omega(s) = K_b \cdot s \theta(s)$$

Where $K_b$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad).

**Step 3 — Mechanical equation (Newton's 2nd law for rotation):**

The motor torque $T_m(s)$ drives the inertia $J$ against viscous friction $b$ and external load torque $T_L(s)$:

$$T_m(s) = (J s + b) \cdot \omega(s) = (J s + b) \cdot s \theta(s)$$

**Step 4 — Motor torque–current relation:**

$$T_m(s) = K_t \cdot I_a(s)$$

Where $K_t$ = torque constant (N·m/A).

**Step 5 — Solve electrical equation for $I_a(s)$:**

$$I_a(s) = \dfrac{V_a(s) - K_b s \theta(s)}{R_a + L_a s}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into mechanical equation:**

$$(J s + b) s \theta(s) = K_t \cdot \dfrac{V_a(s) - K_b s \theta(s)}{R_a + L_a s}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by $(R_a + L_a s)$:**

$$(J s + b) s \theta(s) (R_a + L_a s) = K_t V_a(s) - K_t K_b s \theta(s)$$

**Step 8 — Collect $\theta(s)$ terms on the left:**

$$[(J s + b)(R_a + L_a s) s + K_t K_b s] \theta(s) = K_t V_a(s)$$

**Step 9 — Factor $s$ and isolate the transfer function:**

$$s \left[(J s + b)(R_a + L_a s) + K_t K_b\right] \theta(s) = K_t V_a(s)$$

**Step 10 — Final Transfer Function (Position):**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + b) + K_t K_b\right]}}$$

**Simplification (if $L_a$ is small, often neglected):**

$$\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} \approx \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[R_a (J s + b) + K_t K_b\right]} = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

Where $K = K_t / (R_a b + K_t K_b)$ and $\tau = R_a J / (R_a b + K_t K_b)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The DC motor with position output is a **Type-1 system** (one pole at the origin $s = 0$), which means it can track a step input with **zero steady-state error** when used in unity feedback.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: PID Controller Output Equation

**Step 1 — Time-domain control law:**

The PID controller combines three actions on the error signal $e(t)$:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform of each term (zero initial conditions):**

- Proportional: $\mathcal{L}\{K_p e(t)\} = K_p E(s)$
- Integral: $\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{K_i}{s} E(s)$
- Derivative: $\mathcal{L}\left\{K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = K_d s E(s)$

**Step 3 — Sum the three components:**

$$U(s) = \left(K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Write the controller transfer function:**

$$C(s) = \dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

**Step 5 — Express as a single rational function (multiply numerator and denominator by $s$):**

$$\boxed{C(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}}$$

> This shows the PID has **two zeros** (roots of $K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i = 0$) and **one pole at the origin** ($s = 0$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Controller Gains on Step Response

| Controller | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $K_p$ | Decreases ↓ | Increases ↑ | Small change | Decreases (never zero for P) |
| Increase $K_i$ | Decreases ↓ | Increases ↑ | Increases ↑ | **Eliminated → 0** |
| Increase $K_d$ | Minor change | **Decreases ↓** | Decreases ↓ | No change |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Recall:** $K_p$ → speed, $K_i$ → accuracy, $K_d$ → damping. This 3-word summary is often the key to solving "explain the role of each PID term" questions.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Steady-State Error of P-Controlled Unity Feedback System

**Problem:** For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{5}{s(s+2)}$ and $C(s) = K_p$, find the steady-state error to a unit step input $R(s) = 1/s$.

**Step 1 — Error transfer function for unity feedback:**

$$\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s) C(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{5 K_p}{s(s+2)}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R(s) = 1/s$ and apply Final Value Theorem:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{5 K_p}{s(s+2)}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{5 K_p}{s(s+2)}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limit as $s \to 0$:**
- The denominator term $\dfrac{5 K_p}{s(s+2)} \to \infty$ as $s \to 0$.

$$e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> **Result:** For a **Type-1** system (one pole at origin in $G(s)$) with proportional control and unity feedback, the steady-state error to a **step input is zero** even with a P-controller. The integral-like behavior comes from the plant itself, not the controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often say "P-controller always has steady-state error" — this is **only true for Type-0 plants** (no integrator in the plant). For a DC motor (Type-1), even P-control tracks a step with zero SSE.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Linearity Test on a Robotic Joint Equation

**System:** $\ddot{\theta} + 2 \dot{\theta}^2 + 3 \theta = u(t)$

**Step 1 — Test with two inputs $u_1, u_2$ producing outputs $\theta_1, \theta_2$:**

$$\ddot{\theta_1} + 2 \dot{\theta_1}^2 + 3 \theta_1 = u_1$$
$$\ddot{\theta_2} + 2 \dot{\theta_2}^2 + 3 \theta_2 = u_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the response to the combined input $u_c = a u_1 + b u_2$:**

The output $\theta_c$ of the linear system should equal $a \theta_1 + b \theta_2$:

$$\ddot{\theta_c} + 2 \dot{\theta_c}^2 + 3 \theta_c = a u_1 + b u_2$$

**Step 3 — Check if $\theta_c = a\theta_1 + b\theta_2$ satisfies the equation:**

$$\dfrac{d^2}{dt^2}(a\theta_1 + b\theta_2) + 2 \left[\dfrac{d}{dt}(a\theta_1 + b\theta_2)\right]^2 + 3(a\theta_1 + b\theta_2)$$
$$= a \ddot{\theta_1} + b \ddot{\theta_2} + 2 (a \dot{\theta_1} + b \dot{\theta_2})^2 + 3 a \theta_1 + 3 b \theta_2$$

**Step 4 — Expand the squared term:**

$$(a \dot{\theta_1} + b \dot{\theta_2})^2 = a^2 \dot{\theta_1}^2 + 2ab \dot{\theta_1}\dot{\theta_2} + b^2 \dot{\theta_2}^2$$

This produces $a^2$, $b^2$, and a **cross-term $2ab \dot{\theta_1}\dot{\theta_2}$** that does **not** exist in the desired linear response.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The system is **non-linear** due to the $\dot{\theta}^2$ term, which violates superposition. The squared term is a classic signature of **non-linear dynamics** in robotic manipulators (e.g., Coriolis-like effects in single-DOF approximation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This kind of question tests whether a student can **apply the superposition test** rigorously. Always expand and check for cross-terms or higher powers.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Open-Loop Control System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R([Reference Input r t]) --> C[Controller C s]
    C --> G[Plant G s]
    G --> Y([Output y t])
    style R fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style Y fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style C fill:#B8E0FF,stroke:#003E7E,color:#000
    style G fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Reference $r(t)$ flows **rightward** through controller and plant.
- **No return path** from output to input.
- Output is the **final product** of the chain.

---

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Control System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R([Reference r t]) --> SUM((Sum Junction))
    SUM -->|error e t| C[Controller C s]
    C -->|u t| G[Plant G s]
    G --> Y([Output y t])
    Y -->|measured| H[Sensor H s]
    H --> SUM
    style R fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style Y fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style SUM fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#7E1B1B,color:#000
    style C fill:#B8E0FF,stroke:#003E7E,color:#000
    style G fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style H fill:#E0C8FF,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Summing junction computes $e(t) = r(t) - H(s) y(t)$.
- Controller $C(s)$ (P, PD, or PID) generates $u(t)$.
- Plant $G(s)$ (e.g., DC motor) drives the output.
- Sensor $H(s)$ (encoder, resolver) measures output and closes the loop.

---

### 4.3 PID-Controlled Robotic Joint — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Reference
        REF([Desired Angle theta ref])
    end
    subgraph Feedback
        SENS([Encoder Reading theta actual])
    end
    SUM((Compare -))
    REF --> SUM
    SENS --> SUM
    SUM -->|error e| PID[P I D Controller]
    PID -->|voltage u| AMP[Motor Driver]
    AMP -->|current i| MOTOR[DC Motor Joint]
    MOTOR -->|torque| LOAD[Robot Link]
    LOAD --> POS([Joint Position theta])
    POS --> SENS
    style REF fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style POS fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style PID fill:#B8E0FF,stroke:#003E7E,color:#000
    style SUM fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#7E1B1B,color:#000
    style AMP fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style MOTOR fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style LOAD fill:#D7CCC8,stroke:#3E2723,color:#000
    style SENS fill:#E0C8FF,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **desired** and **actual** joint angles are compared.
- The **error** drives the **PID controller** → **motor driver** → **DC motor** → **link**.
- The **encoder** closes the loop, creating a continuously correcting servo system.

---

### 4.4 Comparison: Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OL[Open Loop System]
        direction LR
        R1([r t]) --> C1[Controller] --> G1[Plant] --> Y1([y t])
    end
    subgraph CL[Closed Loop System]
        direction LR
        R2([r t]) --> SUM2((Sum)) --> C2[Controller] --> G2[Plant] --> Y2([y t])
        Y2 -.->|feedback| H2[Sensor] -.-> SUM2
    end
    style R1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style Y1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style Y2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style SUM2 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#7E1B1B,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed feedback path** in the closed-loop diagram is what makes the system "closed." The open-loop diagram has **no dashed return path**.

---

### 4.5 P / PD / PID Controller Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E([Error e t]) --> KP[Gain Kp]
    KP --> SUMC((Sum))
    E --> KI[Integrator Ki s]
    KI --> SUMC
    E --> KD[Derivator Kd s]
    KD --> SUMC
    SUMC --> U([Control u t])
    style E fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style U fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style KP fill:#B8E0FF,stroke:#003E7E,color:#000
    style KI fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style KD fill:#E0C8FF,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style SUMC fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#7E1B1B,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The error $e(t)$ is fed into **three parallel branches**:
  - Proportional (direct gain $K_p$)
  - Integral (accumulator $K_i/s$)
  - Derivative (rate $K_d s$)
- The three branches are summed to give $u(t)$.
- **Disabling the I-branch** → PD controller.
- **Disabling both I and D branches** → P controller.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with suitable examples from robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low (depends on calibration) | High (self-correcting) |
| Disturbance Rejection | None | Yes |
| Stability | Always stable (no oscillation) | Conditional (may become unstable) |
| Cost | Low (no sensors) | Higher (sensors + wiring) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| **Robotic Example** | Stepper motor driving a printer head | Servo motor with encoder in a robot arm joint |

**Memory Trick:** "Open-loop **trusts**, closed-loop **checks**."

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A clean **comparison table** + one example per system = full 3 marks. Avoid vague "it is better/worse" answers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define transfer function. State any two assumptions made in deriving it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The transfer function of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, assuming **all initial conditions are zero**:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$$

**Assumptions (any two):**
1. The system is **linear** (obeys superposition).
2. The system is **time-invariant** (parameters do not change with time).
3. **All initial conditions are zero**.
4. The system is **causal** (output depends only on present and past inputs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common mistake: students write $G(s) = Y(s) / U(s)$ without specifying the **zero initial condition** — this costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop control system** architecture. Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** for a unity negative feedback system. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a robotic joint driven by a DC motor, derive the **transfer function** relating output position to input voltage. Identify the type of the system and comment on its **steady-state error** to a step input. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
[See Section 4.2 — Closed-loop diagram with reference, summing junction, controller $C(s)$, plant $G(s)$, sensor $H(s)$, and feedback path.]

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Given unity negative feedback ($H(s) = 1$).

Error signal:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$

Controller output:

$$U(s) = C(s) E(s)$$

Plant output:

$$Y(s) = G(s) U(s)$$

Substitute $U(s)$:

$$Y(s) = G(s) C(s) E(s) = G(s) C(s) [R(s) - Y(s)]$$

Rearrange:

$$Y(s) + G(s) C(s) Y(s) = G(s) C(s) R(s)$$
$$Y(s) [1 + G(s) C(s)] = G(s) C(s) R(s)$$

**Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}}$$

> **Examiner's note:** Full marks require **both** the diagram and the step-by-step algebraic manipulation, not just the final expression.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]**

**Armature voltage equation (1 mark):**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b s \theta(s)$$

**Mechanical torque equation (1 mark):**

$$T_m(s) = (J s + b) s \theta(s) = K_t I_a(s)$$

**Solve electrical for current (1 mark):**

$$I_a(s) = \dfrac{V_a(s) - K_b s \theta(s)}{R_a + L_a s}$$

**Substitute and simplify (2 marks):**

$$(Js + b) s \theta(s) (R_a + L_a s) = K_t V_a(s) - K_t K_b s \theta(s)$$

$$s \left[(Js + b)(R_a + L_a s) + K_t K_b\right] \theta(s) = K_t V_a(s)$$

**Final TF (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + b) + K_t K_b\right]}$$

**Type and steady-state error (1 mark):**
- **Type-1 system** (one pole at the origin).
- In unity feedback, $e_{ss}$ for a step input is **zero** (Type-1 property).
- This is why P-control suffices for DC motor position control without integral action.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working of **P, PD, and PID controllers** with mathematical expressions and block diagrams. Compare their effect on **rise time, overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error**. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is meant by **linear** and **non-linear** control systems? Apply the **superposition test** to determine whether the system $\ddot{y} + 2\dot{y} + y^2 = u(t)$ is linear or non-linear. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]**

**P-Controller (2 marks):**
- Law: $u(t) = K_p e(t)$; TF: $C(s) = K_p$
- Block: Error → $K_p$ → $u(t)$.
- Effect: Speeds up response, but **cannot eliminate** SSE for Type-0 plants.

**PD-Controller (2 marks):**
- Law: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$; TF: $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$
- Block: Error splits into two parallel branches ($K_p$ and $K_d s$).
- Effect: Adds **damping**, reduces overshoot, does not eliminate SSE.

**PID-Controller (2 marks):**
- Law: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$
- TF: $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$
- Block: Three parallel branches summed.
- Effect: Eliminates SSE due to integral term.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | ↑$K_p$ | ↑$K_i$ | ↑$K_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise Time | ↓ | ↓ | Minor change |
| Overshoot | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Settling Time | Minor ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Steady-State Error | ↓ (not zero) | → 0 | No effect |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- A **linear** system satisfies **superposition** (additivity + homogeneity).
- A **non-linear** system violates superposition — it has non-linear terms like $y^2$, $\dot{y}^2$, $\sin(y)$, etc.

**Superposition Test (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply $u_1(t) \to y_1(t)$:

$$\ddot{y_1} + 2\dot{y_1} + y_1^2 = u_1(t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply $u_2(t) \to y_2(t)$:

$$\ddot{y_2} + 2\dot{y_2} + y_2^2 = u_2(t)$$

**Step 3:** Combined input: $u_c(t) = a u_1(t) + b u_2(t)$.
Assume linear response: $y_c = a y_1 + b y_2$.

**Step 4:** Substitute and check:

$$\ddot{y_c} + 2\dot{y_c} + y_c^2 = a\ddot{y_1} + b\ddot{y_2} + 2(a\dot{y_1} + b\dot{y_2}) + (a y_1 + b y_2)^2$$

Expand the squared term:

$$(a y_1 + b y_2)^2 = a^2 y_1^2 + 2ab y_1 y_2 + b^2 y_2^2$$

For linearity we would need $a y_1^2 + b y_2^2$ but we got **$a^2 y_1^2 + 2ab y_1 y_2 + b^2 y_2^2$** — which contains the **cross-term $2ab y_1 y_2$** and squared coefficients $a^2, b^2$.

**Step 5:** These extra terms mean the system **violates superposition**.

**Conclusion:** The system $\ddot{y} + 2\dot{y} + y^2 = u(t)$ is **NON-LINEAR** due to the $y^2$ term.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **MUST show expansion** of squared/cross terms — do not just state "it has $y^2$, so it is non-linear." Marks go to the **demonstration of violation**.
> 2. **Do not confuse** "linear controller" with "linear system" — a PID is a linear controller, but the overall closed-loop may be non-linear if the plant is non-linear.
> 3. **In Q.A(b)**, students often forget to **state the system type** (Type-1) and its implication for steady-state error — this loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

**Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop:**
- Open-loop = **no feedback**, simple, cheap, inaccurate, no disturbance rejection.
- Closed-loop = **feedback present**, accurate, robust, may become unstable.
- Robotic examples: stepper (open) vs servo with encoder (closed).

**Transfer Function:**
- $G(s) = Y(s) / U(s)$, **zero initial conditions**, **LTI systems only**.
- Closed-loop TF (unity feedback): $\dfrac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}$.
- Characteristic equation: $1 + G(s) C(s) = 0$ → roots determine stability.

**P Controller:**
- $C(s) = K_p$, reduces rise time, **leaves steady-state error** (for Type-0 plants).

**PD Controller:**
- $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$, adds damping, **reduces overshoot**, no SSE elimination.
- **Workhorse of robotic joint control** when model uncertainty is low.

**PID Controller:**
- $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$, eliminates SSE due to integral term.
- **Tuning methods:** Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, manual trial.
- **Anti-windup** needed when actuator saturates.

**DC Motor Position TF:**
- $\theta(s)/V_a(s) = \dfrac{K_t}{s[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + b) + K_t K_b]}$
- **Type-1 system** → tracks step with **zero SSE** even with P-controller in unity feedback.

**Linear vs Non-Linear:**
- **Linearity test:** apply $a u_1 + b u_2$ → output must be $a y_1 + b y_2$.
- **Non-linearity indicators:** squared terms, products, trig functions, saturation, dead-zone.
- **Robotic manipulator dynamics are inherently non-linear** (centrifugal, Coriolis, gravity coupling).

**Stability Quick-Check:**
- **Routh-Hurwitz** for characteristic polynomial of closed-loop system.
- For $G(s) = 1/[s(s+a)]$ with P-control $K_p$: stable if $a > 0$ and $K_p > 0$.

**Cross-Cutting Concepts to Remember:**
- **Disturbance rejection** = a key reason for choosing closed-loop.
- **Sensitivity to parameter variation** = reduced by feedback.
- **Bandwidth vs stability** = trade-off; increasing $K_p$ widens bandwidth but may cause oscillation.
- **Sensor noise** is amplified by $K_d$ → low-pass filter on derivative term in practice.
- **Anti-windup** is essential for real PID with saturating actuators (common in robots).
- **Computed-torque control** is a non-linear method for manipulator arms that cancels the non-linear dynamics and applies a linear servo in joint space.

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Open-loop trusts, closed-loop checks. P gives speed, I gives accuracy, D gives damping. Robots are non-linear, but we pretend they're linear — locally."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Hardware and Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT743 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Control Hardware and Interfacing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Control Hardware and Interfacing** in a robotic system refers to the integrated assembly of physical electronic, electromechanical, and computational components that enable a controller to perceive, process, and actuate on the real world. It encompasses the **sensors** (which capture physical state variables), **signal-conditioning circuits** (which normalize raw signals), **controllers** (microcontroller, PLC, or industrial PC that execute control algorithms), **actuators and motor drivers** (which convert electrical commands into mechanical motion), and the **interfacing buses/protocols** (which establish reliable bidirectional data transfer between subsystems). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes the *closed-loop architecture* — the synergy between perception (sensors), cognition (microcontroller/PLC), and action (drivers/actuators). Students must understand not only individual components but also the signal-flow chain that binds them into a working robotic cell. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a human body performing a routine task — say, picking up a hot cup of coffee: - **Sensors** = Your *fingertip nerves* (temperature, pressure) and *eyes* (vision) gather information. - **Signal Conditioning** = The *spinal cord*, which amplifies and filters nerve signals before they reach the brain. - **Controller (Microcontroller/PLC)** = The *brain*, which interprets the signal, decides whether to grip tighter or release, and sends commands. - **Driver Circuit** = The *motor neurons*, which translate the brain's electrical intent into muscle-fiber activation signals. - **Actuator** = Your *arm muscles*, which physically execute the motion. - **Interface Bus (e.g., I²C, SPI, CAN)** = The *nervous system network* carrying messages to and fro. Just as a damaged nerve breaks the body’s coordination, a poorly designed interface (mismatched voltage levels, noisy analog signals, or wrong baud-rate) breaks the robot’s coordination. ### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Metrics The following standard metrics must be memorized for KTU examinations: - **TTL Logic Level High:** $\geq \mathbf{2.0\ V}$ - **TTL Logic Level Low:** $\leq \mathbf{0.8\ V}$ - **CMOS Logic Level High:** $\geq \mathbf{0.7 \times V_{DD}}$ - **Industrial Standard:** **0–10 V** analog or **4–20 mA** current loop - **Controller Clock Speed range:** **1 MHz – 200 MHz** (typical microcontroller/PLC scan) - **I²C bus speed:** $\mathbf{100\ kHz}$ (Standard), $\mathbf{400\ kHz}$ (Fast), $\mathbf{3.4\ MHz}$ (High-Speed) - **CAN bus speed:** up to **1 Mbps** > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Industrial environments favor the **4–20 mA current loop** over voltage signaling because current is unaffected by cable resistance, voltage drops, and electromagnetic noise — a key interview and viva question. ### 1.4 Block-Level Classification of Control Hardware A robotics control system can be classified into **five functional blocks**: 1. **Perception Layer** — Encoders, resolvers, tactile sensors, force/torque sensors, LiDAR, IMU, cameras. 2. **Signal Conditioning Layer** — Amplifiers (e.g., INA128 instrumentation amp), filters (RC, active), ADC drivers, optocouplers. 3. **Controller Layer** — Microcontroller (Arduino, STM32, ESP32), PLC (Allen-Bradley, Siemens S7), DSP, or SoC (Raspberry Pi, Jetson). 4. **Driver / Power Electronics Layer** — H-bridge motor drivers (L298N, BTS7960), servo drivers, VFDs, MOSFET/IGBT gate drivers. 5. **Actuator Layer** — DC motors, stepper motors, BLDC, servo motors, pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Signal Flow Chain The control hardware chain follows a **strictly linear closed-loop signal flow**. Each stage has a defined responsibility: - **Step 1: Sensing** — A transducer converts a physical quantity (e.g., displacement, force) into an electrical signal (resistance, voltage, capacitance change). - **Step 2: Transduction** — The raw electrical signal is converted to a standardized electrical quantity (e.g., a strain gauge's $\Delta R / R$ becomes a voltage via a Wheatstone bridge). - **Step 3: Analog Signal Conditioning** — Amplification (raising the mV-range signal to 0–5 V), Filtering (anti-aliasing low-pass filter), Isolation (optocoupler or transformer isolation for safety). - **Step 4: Digitization** — An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** samples the conditioned signal at or above the Nyquist rate. - **Step 5: Processing** — The digital value is read by the microcontroller/PLC; the control algorithm (PID, fuzzy, neural) computes a corrective command. - **Step 6: Command Output** — The controller issues a digital command (PWM, pulse train, or serial message). - **Step 7: Driver Stage** — A power driver amplifies the low-power command to the high-current/voltage required by the actuator. - **Step 8: Actuation** — The actuator produces mechanical work. - **Step 9: Feedback** — The new mechanical state is sensed, closing the loop. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage - **Why amplify?** Sensors often output signals in the **mV range**, while ADCs need **0–5 V**. Amplification improves **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. - **Why filter?** Real-world signals contain high-frequency noise; low-pass filters prevent **aliasing** during ADC sampling. - **Why isolate?** Industrial environments have ground loops, surges, and EMI. Isolation protects the **low-voltage logic side** from the **high-voltage power side**. - **Why PWM for DC motors?** Varying the average DC voltage by rapidly switching the supply is more **efficient** (the transistor is either fully ON or fully OFF, minimizing heat dissipation). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---| | ADC Resolution | $N = \log_2(2^n)$ levels for $n$-bit ADC | $n = 8, 10, 12, 16$ bits typical | | Quantization Step | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts per LSB | | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz; *must exceed twice the highest signal frequency* | | Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_o = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)$ | Balanced when $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ | | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq D \leq 1$ | | Average Motor Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | Volts | | Instrumentation Amp Gain | $G = 1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_{gain}}$ | Set by single external resistor | | PID Controller Output | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Sum of three corrective terms | | Baud Rate (UART) | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud}}$ | Seconds per bit | | Current Loop Conversion | $I = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{R_{sense}}$ where $V_{sense} = 4\text{–}20\text{ mA}$ corresponds to $0\text{–}100\%$ | Use **$R = 250\ \Omega$** to get **1–5 V** output | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Robotic Arms (e.g., FANUC, ABB):** Use **servo drives** with resolver feedback, controlled by a PLC over **EtherCAT** — a real-time industrial Ethernet protocol. - **Mobile Robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics Spot, TurtleBot):** Use **microcontroller + SoC hybrid** — STM32 for low-level motor control, NVIDIA Jetson for high-level perception. - **CNC Machines:** Use **stepper motors** with **microstepping drivers** for precise positioning; **closed-loop via optical encoders**. - **HVAC and Process Control:** Use **4–20 mA current loops** to transmit sensor readings over **hundreds of meters** without signal degradation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — ADC Resolution and Quantization Error **Problem Context:** A robotic force sensor outputs an analog signal in the range **0–10 V**. The controller uses a **12-bit ADC** with a reference voltage of **5 V**. Determine: (a) the number of discrete levels, (b) the voltage resolution per LSB, and (c) the maximum quantization error. **Step 1 — Number of Discrete Levels:** A $n$-bit ADC produces $2^n$ discrete binary codes. Since the binary code "0" represents the lowest voltage and the code "$2^n - 1$" represents the highest, the *total number of intervals* is $2^n - 1$. $$ N_{levels} = 2^{12} = 4096\ \text{levels} $$ **Step 2 — Quantization Step (LSB Voltage):** The quantization step (voltage represented by the Least Significant Bit) is given by: $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} $$ Substituting $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V}$ and $n = 12$: $$ \Delta V = \frac{5}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{5}{4095} \approx 1.221 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} $$ So $\Delta V \approx 1.221\ \text{mV}$ per LSB. **Step 3 — Maximum Quantization Error:** Theoretically, an analog value can fall anywhere within a quantization interval of width $\Delta V$, so the maximum deviation from the true value is half the LSB: $$ E_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} \approx \pm 0.6105\ \text{mV} $$ **Step 4 — Converting the Force-Sensor Reading:** Suppose the sensor outputs $V_{in} = 3.7\ \text{V}$. The corresponding digital count is: $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3.7}{0.001221} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3030.30 \right\rfloor = 3030 $$ In hexadecimal (useful for embedded coding): $3030_{10} = \text{BD6}_{16}$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** When the analog input exceeds $V_{ref}$, the ADC *saturates* at the maximum count ($4095$). Hence, for a 10 V sensor signal, an external **voltage divider** must scale it down to $\leq 5\ \text{V}$ before reaching the ADC pin. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Designing a Voltage Divider for ADC Input Scaling **Problem Context:** A potentiometric position sensor outputs **0–12 V**. The microcontroller ADC accepts **0–3.3 V**. Design a voltage divider to safely interface them. **Step 1 — Define the Scaling Ratio:** We need the divider to map $V_{in,max} = 12\ \text{V}$ to $V_{out,max} = 3.3\ \text{V}$. $$ \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{3.3}{12} = 0.275 $$ **Step 2 — Choose $R_2$ and Solve for $R_1$:** Choose a standard $R_2 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (limits current to a safe value). $$ R_1 = R_2 \left( \frac{1}{0.275} - 1 \right) = 10000 \cdot (2.6364) \approx 26.36\ \text{k}\Omega $$ Standard closest value: $R_1 = 27\ \text{k}\Omega$. **Step 3 — Verify the New Output Voltage at Maximum Input:** $$ V_{out,max} = V_{in,max} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \frac{10}{27 + 10} = 12 \cdot \frac{10}{37} \approx 3.243\ \text{V} $$ This is safely below 3.3 V, leaving a **2 % safety margin** against component tolerance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the safety margin?** Resistors have tolerances (typically $\pm 5\%$). If $R_2$ is at its maximum and $R_1$ at its minimum, the output could exceed 3.3 V and damage the ADC. Always derate. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — PWM Duty Cycle to Motor Speed **Problem Context:** A 24 V DC gear motor has a no-load speed of **3000 RPM** at rated voltage. The microcontroller produces a PWM signal with $V_{supply} = 24\ \text{V}$ and duty cycle $D = 0.4$. **Step 1 — Average Voltage Applied:** $$ V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply} = 0.4 \cdot 24 = 9.6\ \text{V} $$ **Step 2 — Assuming Linear V–ω Relation (Approximation):** $$ \omega = \omega_{NL} \cdot \frac{V_{avg}}{V_{rated}} = 3000 \cdot \frac{9.6}{24} = 1200\ \text{RPM} $$ **Step 3 — In Practice (Including Voltage Drop $V_{drop}$ and Friction):** A more accurate model: $$ \omega = K_v \cdot (V_{avg} - I_a R_a - V_{brush}) $$ Where $K_v$ is the motor velocity constant, $I_a$ is armature current, $R_a$ is armature resistance, and $V_{brush}$ is the brush-contact voltage drop. --- ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Sampling Rate for Encoder Feedback **Problem Context:** A quadrature encoder on a motor shaft produces **500 pulses per revolution**. The motor rotates at **1800 RPM**. Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid missing position transitions. **Step 1 — Pulses per Second:** $$ f_{pulse} = \frac{500 \cdot 1800}{60} = 15000\ \text{Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Quadrature Multiplication:** Quadrature decoding yields **4× the pulse count** (rising + falling edges of both channels): $$ f_{effective} = 4 \cdot 15000 = 60000\ \text{Hz} = 60\ \text{kHz} $$ **Step 3 — Nyquist Minimum:** $$ f_{sample} \geq 2 \cdot f_{effective} = 120\ \text{kHz} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often use only $f_{pulse}$ instead of $4 \cdot f_{pulse}$ for quadrature, leading to an undersampled system and aliasing in the velocity estimate. Always use the *effective count after quadrature decoding*. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Interfacing Protocols | Parameter | UART | SPI | I²C | CAN | |---|---|---|---|---| | Topology | Point-to-point | Master-Slave (multi-slave) | Multi-Master bus | Multi-Master bus | | Speed | Up to 1 Mbps | Up to 50 Mbps | 100 kbps – 3.4 Mbps | Up to 1 Mbps | | Wires | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 2 (CAN-H, CAN-L) | | Max Devices | 2 | Many (limited by CS pins) | 127 (by address) | Many (by ID filtering) | | Noise Immunity | Low | Low | Low | **Very High (differential)** | | Typical Use | Debug, GPS, BT | SD card, displays, sensors | EEPROM, RTC, IMU | Automotive, industrial | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Robotic Control Hardware Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
Position Force Temp Vision] --> B[Sensors and Transducers] B --> C[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier Filter Isolation] C --> D[ADC and Digital Front End] D --> E[Controller
Microcontroller or PLC] E --> F[PWM or Command Generator] F --> G[Motor Driver
H Bridge or VFD] G --> H[Actuators
DC Stepper Servo BLDC] H --> I[Mechanical Load
Joint Link Wheel] I -.Feedback.-> B subgraph Perception B C D end subgraph Decision E F end subgraph Action G H I end ``` ### 4.2 Sensor Interfacing — Analog Sensor to Microcontroller ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Physical Stimulus] --> SG[Sensor Element
e.g. Strain Gauge] SG --> WB[Wheatstone Bridge] WB --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain G] IA --> LPF[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter
fc = 1 / 2piRC] LPF --> ADC[ADC Input Pin] ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller] MCU --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing] DSP --> OUT[Control Output] ``` ### 4.3 Motor Driver Interfacing — H-Bridge Control ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU[Microcontroller GPIO] -->|PWM Signal| DRV[Motor Driver IC
L298N or BTS7960] DRV -->|High Current Switching| M[DC Motor] M -->|Back EMF| FB[Encoder or Back EMF Sensor] FB --> MCU PSU[Power Supply 12V or 24V] --> DRV VCC[Logic Supply 5V or 3.3V] --> MCU ``` ### 4.4 Communication Protocol Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New Interfacing Task] --> Q1{Distance over 10 m?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Industrial Environment?} Q1 -->|No| Q3{Single Device?} Q2 -->|Yes| CAN[Use CAN or RS485] Q2 -->|No| UART_L[Use UART with shielding] Q3 -->|Yes| UART[Use UART] Q3 -->|No| Q4{High Speed Required?} Q4 -->|Yes| SPI[Use SPI] Q4 -->|No| Q5{Low Pin Count Desired?} Q5 -->|Yes| I2C[Use I2C] Q5 -->|No| SPI2[Use SPI] ``` ### 4.5 Closed-Loop Position Control — PID Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Reference Position] --> SUM((Sum)) SUM -->|Error e t| PID[PID Controller
Kp Ki Kd] PID --> U[Control Signal u t] U --> PLANT[Plant
Motor and Load] PLANT --> Y[Output Position] Y --> FB[Feedback Sensor
Encoder] FB --> SUM ``` > [!NOTE] > **PID Tuning Intuition:** > - **$K_p$** (Proportional): Reacts to *current error*. Too high → oscillation. > - **$K_i$** (Integral): Eliminates *steady-state error*. Too high → slow response / overshoot. > - **$K_d$** (Derivative): Dampens *rate of change*. Too high → amplifies sensor noise. > > **KTU Viva Classic:** "How do you tune a PID controller empirically?" — Answer: Use the **Ziegler-Nichols method** or **manual heuristic**: increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation, then halve $K_p$ and add $K_i$ to remove offset, then add $K_d$ to damp. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Q1.** Distinguish between **open-loop** and **closed-loop** control systems in robotics. Give one robotic example of each. **Model Answer:** - **Open-loop control** has no feedback; the controller issues a command and assumes the actuator reaches the desired state. Example: a *stepper-motor-driven 3D printer* — it sends $N$ pulses and trusts the motor moved exactly $N$ steps. - **Closed-loop control** continuously measures the actual output and corrects the command. Example: a *servo motor with encoder feedback* in a robotic arm joint — the encoder measures position and the controller corrects any deviation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "feedback" as the difference. KTU examiners expect the *consequence*: open-loop cannot correct for *load disturbances*, while closed-loop *can*. Mention this for full marks. --- **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Q2.** What is a **4–20 mA current loop**? Why is it preferred over a 0–10 V voltage signal in industrial instrumentation? **Model Answer:** The 4–20 mA current loop is an analog signaling standard where the transmitter regulates current between 4 mA (representing 0 % of the measured range) and 20 mA (representing 100 %). It is preferred because: 1. **Immune to cable resistance and voltage drop** — current is constant regardless of wire length (up to a few hundred meters). 2. **Live-zero detection** — a current of 0 mA indicates a *broken wire*, not a zero reading, enabling fault diagnostics. 3. **High noise immunity** — current signals are far less affected by electromagnetic interference than voltage signals. 4. **Intrinsic safety** — limited current makes it suitable for hazardous (explosive) environments. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU End Semester Examination Pattern — Module 4, 14-Mark Question, Internal Choice A or B]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Robot Sensor Signal Conditioning* **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the various stages of signal conditioning required to interface a **strain-gauge-based force sensor** with a microcontroller ADC. Derive the expression for the output voltage of a **Wheatstone bridge** when one arm resistance changes by $\Delta R$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A strain gauge with gauge factor $G_f = 2.1$ and nominal resistance $R = 350\ \Omega$ is bonded to a cantilever beam. The beam experiences a strain $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon$. The gauge is connected in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge supplied by $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$, and the other three arms are $350\ \Omega$ fixed resistors. The bridge output is fed to an instrumentation amplifier with gain $G = 250$. Determine: (i) $\Delta R$, (ii) bridge output voltage, (iii) amplifier output voltage. --- ##### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Solution:** **Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid for visual).** The signal-conditioning stages are: 1. **Wheatstone Bridge** — converts $\Delta R$ into a differential voltage. 2. **Instrumentation Amplifier** — amplifies the millivolt-level differential signal to a 0–5 V range. 3. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** — restricts bandwidth below half the ADC sampling rate. 4. **Level Shifter (if needed)** — shifts bipolar $\pm V$ output to unipolar 0–$V_{ref}$. 5. **ADC Interface** — analog signal fed to microcontroller ADC pin. **Wheatstone Bridge Derivation:** Let the four arms be $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$, and excitation be $V_{ex}$. The two midpoints give the differential output: $$ V_a = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ $$ V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} $$ $$ V_o = V_a - V_b = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right) $$ For a single active gauge: $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. $$ V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R}{R+\Delta R + R} - \frac{R}{R + R} \right) $$ $$ V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right) $$ For small $\Delta R \ll R$: $$ V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R}{4R} $$ The negative sign indicates polarity; the magnitude is: $$ \vert V_o \vert \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4 R} $$ --- **(b) Solution:** **(i) Change in Resistance:** The gauge factor relates strain to resistance change: $$ G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} $$ $$ \Delta R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot R = 2.1 \cdot (500 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 350 $$ $$ \Delta R = 2.1 \cdot 0.0005 \cdot 350 = 0.3675\ \Omega $$ **(ii) Bridge Output Voltage:** $$ \vert V_o \vert = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4 R} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.3675}{4 \cdot 350} $$ $$ \vert V_o \vert = \frac{1.8375}{1400} \approx 1.3125 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 1.31\ \text{mV} $$ **(iii) Amplifier Output Voltage:** $$ V_{amp} = G \cdot \vert V_o \vert = 250 \cdot 1.3125 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.328\ \text{V} $$ This is now in a comfortable range for a 0–5 V ADC. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **factor of 4** in the Wheatstone bridge formula (mistaking it for a simple voltage divider). They also forget to **multiply the gauge factor by strain** before substituting. Both are common 1-mark deductions. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Motor Driver and PWM Interfacing* **(a) [7 Marks]** With a circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **H-bridge motor driver** for bidirectional control of a DC motor. Why are PWM signals used instead of analog voltage for speed control? **(b) [7 Marks]** A robotic wheel is driven by a 24 V DC motor with armature resistance $R_a = 1.2\ \Omega$. The H-bridge produces a PWM waveform at 25 kHz with duty cycle $D = 0.65$ and supply $V_s = 24\ \text{V}$. The motor draws an average current of $I_a = 3.5\ \text{A}$. Compute: (i) the average voltage across the motor, (ii) the average back-EMF, (iii) the speed in RPM if the motor constant $K_v = 45\ \text{RPM/V}$, (iv) the approximate mechanical power developed. --- ##### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Solution:** **H-Bridge Operation:** An H-bridge consists of **four switching elements** (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 — typically MOSFETs or BJTs) arranged in an "H" shape around the motor. The motor sits across the bridge's center. - **Forward mode:** Q1 and Q4 are ON, Q2 and Q3 are OFF. Current flows from $V^+$ → Q1 → motor → Q4 → ground. Motor turns clockwise. - **Reverse mode:** Q2 and Q3 are ON, Q1 and Q4 are OFF. Current reverses direction. Motor turns counter-clockwise. - **Brake (Dynamic):** Both high-side or both low-side switches ON. Motor terminals are shorted; back-EMF dissipates rapidly. - **Coast:** All switches OFF. Motor freewheels until friction stops it. **Why PWM?** 1. **Efficiency:** Transistors operate in saturation (fully ON) or cutoff (fully OFF). Power dissipation $P = V_{CE} \cdot I$ is minimal in both states. 2. **No bulky linear regulator** — small MOSFETs replace heat-generating resistors. 3. **Precise digital control** — duty cycle is set by a microcontroller timer; easy to vary. 4. **High switching frequency** (>20 kHz) is *above human hearing*, eliminating audible motor whine. --- **(b) Solution:** **(i) Average Voltage Across Motor:** $$ V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.65 \cdot 24 = 15.6\ \text{V} $$ **(ii) Average Back-EMF:** Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law around the motor loop: $$ V_{avg} = E_b + I_a R_a $$ $$ E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a = 15.6 - (3.5 \cdot 1.2) = 15.6 - 4.2 = 11.4\ \text{V} $$ **(iii) Motor Speed:** $$ N = K_v \cdot E_b = 45 \cdot 11.4 = 513\ \text{RPM} $$ **(iv) Mechanical Power Developed:** Mechanical power equals electrical power delivered minus copper losses: $$ P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 11.4 \cdot 3.5 = 39.9\ \text{W} $$ Alternatively: $$ P_{cu} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a = (3.5)^2 \cdot 1.2 = 14.7\ \text{W} $$ $$ P_{elec} = V_{avg} \cdot I_a = 15.6 \cdot 3.5 = 54.6\ \text{W} $$ $$ P_{mech} = P_{elec} - P_{cu} = 54.6 - 14.7 = 39.9\ \text{W} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $N = K_v \cdot V_{avg}$ instead of $K_v \cdot E_b$. The correct formula uses *back-EMF*, not applied voltage. Also, students forget to subtract $I_a R_a$ — losing 2 marks easily. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - [ ] **Control hardware chain**: Sensor → Condition → ADC → Controller → Driver → Actuator → Feedback. - [ ] **ADC resolution**: $2^n$ levels; LSB voltage $= V_{ref}/(2^n - 1)$. - [ ] **Nyquist criterion**: Sample at *at least twice* the highest signal frequency. - [ ] **Wheatstone bridge** single active gauge: $V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$. - [ ] **Gauge factor**: $G_f = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$. - [ ] **Instrumentation amplifier gain**: $G = 1 + (2R_1 / R_{gain})$. - [ ] **H-bridge modes**: Forward, Reverse, Brake, Coast. - [ ] **PWM average voltage**: $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$. - [ ] **Back-EMF formula**: $E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a$. - [ ] **Motor speed**: $N = K_v \cdot E_b$ (RPM), where $K_v$ is in RPM/V. - [ ] **Quadrature encoder**: Multiply pulse frequency by **4** for effective resolution. - [ ] **4–20 mA loop**: Live-zero (4 mA = 0 %, 0 mA = fault). - [ ] **Protocol selection**: UART (point-to-point), SPI (fast), I²C (low pins), CAN (industrial noise). - [ ] **PID tuning**: Increase $K_p$ → oscillation → halve → add $K_i$ → add $K_d$. - [ ] **Voltage divider safety margin**: Always leave ≥ 5 % margin below ADC's $V_{ref}$. - [ ] **Optocoupler isolation**: Protects logic side from high-voltage power transients. - [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter cutoff**: $f_c \leq f_s / 2$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Mantra:** "Read the question carefully — distinguish *open-loop* from *closed-loop*, *active* from *passive* sensors, *back-EMF* from *applied voltage*." Examiners design options to test exactly these distinctions.
